items.title: Commit small, commit often, push regularly
items.avoid: Working for a week and dropping a 4000-line commit at the end.
items.prefer: One commit per coherent change; push at least daily so your work survives a laptop drop.
items.why: Small commits are easy to review and easy to bisect; pushed commits survive disk failure.
items.example: Aim for commits a reviewer can grasp in <2 minutes.

items.title: Use the imperative voice in the subject line
items.prefer: 'Fix retry logic for 429 responses', 'Add tests for chunker', 'Refactor logger'.
items.why: Imperative reads as 'this commit will [verb]' — matches Git's own output ('Merge branch...', 'Revert...').
items.example:
```
Add retry on 429 from embedding API

The upstream rate-limits us...
```

items.title: Branch names follow a convention
items.why: Consistent prefixes make filtering and tooling (CI, branch protection) easy.

items.title: Write a thorough `.gitignore` from day one
items.prefer: Commit a starter `.gitignore` as one of the first commits (use github/gitignore templates).
items.why: Secrets and large binaries are hard to scrub after the fact; prevention is cheap.

items.title: Use `--force-with-lease` instead of `--force`
items.prefer: `git push --force-with-lease` — refuses if someone else has pushed in the meantime.
items.why: Plain `--force` silently overwrites teammates' work; `--with-lease` makes you re-pull first.
